title: Editing a Macro
subtitle: Paste it, fix it, yank it back. Macros are text.
summary: Because a macro is just register contents, you can paste it into the buffer, edit any keystroke, and yank it back. This is how macros get refined without a full re-record.

blocks.text: Recording is the easy way to *get* a macro. But re-recording every time you want a small fix is painful. Faster: paste the macro into a scratch buffer, edit the keystrokes, yank it back. The macro is text.

Suppose macro `a` is *almost* right but has a bug.

  1. Open a scratch line: {key:o}{key:Esc}.
  2. Paste the macro: {key:"}{key:a}{key:p}. The keystrokes appear as text.
  3. Edit them — replace `dawi` with `daWi`, fix a typo, whatever.
  4. Select the corrected text (e.g. {key:0}{key:v}{key:$} or just place cursor at start) and yank it back into `a`: {key:"}{key:a}{key:y}{key:$}.
  5. Undo the buffer changes ({key:u}, {key:u}) so the keystroke text doesn't pollute your file.
  6. {key:@}{key:a} — try the corrected macro.

Special keys appear as raw escape sequences. {key:<Esc>} shows as `^[`. You can leave them alone and edit only the visible keys around them. To insert a literal {key:<Esc>} in your editing, use {key:Ctrl-V}{key:Esc} — that types the byte without leaving Insert mode.

blocks.title: Scripting the Macro Directly
blocks.text:
If you'd rather skip the paste-edit-yank dance, set the register from the command line:

  • {key::}{key:l}{key:e}{key:t} {key:@}{key:a}{key:=}"^[ciwhello^["
  • {key::}{key:l}{key:e}{key:t} {key:@}{key:a} {key:.}{key:=}" `j`"  *(append a `j` motion)*

In the string form, you can use `\<Esc>` and similar named keys if `'cpoptions'` is set right; or paste literal escape characters with {key:Ctrl-V}{key:Esc} while typing the {key::}{key:l}{key:e}{key:t}.

For a longer-lived macro, consider stashing it in your config. {key::}{key:l}{key:e}{key:t} {key:g}{key::}macros = { 'a': '...' } makes them programmatic; mapping {key:@}{key:a} to a function gives them names.
